title: Violating the Dead and Oil Money
body:
Medical research and education1914–1942

Modern Chinese medical research and education, on the other hand, was an American import, paid for in Standard Oil money. China was a deliberate choice, reflecting the Rockefeller family’s longstanding ties to American Baptist missionary networks already running medical work there and the Foundation’s broader chartered mission to promote “the well-being of mankind throughout the world.”[[cite:rockarch-foundation-history]] 

The Rockefeller Foundation’s China Medical Board had been established in 1914 with a $12 million endowment, modeled on the Johns Hopkins approach that had recently reformed American medical education--research-university affiliation, full-time clinical faculty, and two years of lab-based basic-science training before clinical work, a stricter standard than the missionary teaching hospitals and apprenticeship traditions then common in China.[[cite:rockarch-pumc-history]] Its adviser Simon Flexner called the project that became PUMC “the Johns Hopkins of China.”[[cite:rockfdn-pumc-flexner-quote]] In 1915, the Board purchased the Union Medical College from the London Missionary Society and rebuilt it on the site of a former royal palace in Beijing. 

Formally established in 1917, Peking Union Medical College was conceived as nothing less than “a medical school and hospital comparable with that of the leading institutions of Western civilization.”[[cite:rockarch-pumc-history]] Its small graduating classes, capped at thirty to fifty students, produced an outsized share of the country’s first generation of Western-trained medical scientists. The Harvard-trained biochemist Hsien Wu (Wu Xian) became the first Chinese to head a PUMC department, organizing its biochemistry section in 1924, and is generally credited as the founder of Chinese biochemistry as a research discipline.[[cite:zheng-hsien-wu-2012]][[cite:pmc-cams-pumc-2020]]

End of the Republican model1942–1951

The Republican-era model didn’t survive the war or the political transition that followed it. Japanese forces seized PUMC in January 1942, shortly after Pearl Harbor, and shut it down for the duration of the war; medical research elsewhere scattered as scientists fled inland with whatever equipment they could carry. In January 1951, the new People’s Republic nationalized the institution, ending the era of American-funded Chinese medical education.[[cite:pumc-wikipedia]]

title: The Cultural Revolution, Insulin, and Artemisinin
body:
The [[entity:cas|Chinese Academy of Sciences]], founded in 1949, was modeled on its Soviet counterpart and inherited Soviet biology. The most damaging import was Lysenkoism, the agricultural pseudoscience of Trofim Lysenko, who had won Stalin’s favor in the 1930s by claiming that acquired traits could be inherited[[note:aka Lamarckism]] and that Mendelian genetics was a bourgeois fraud (eugenics, a tale as old as time). Lysenko 

Alternative genetic theories were permitted only after Khrushchev’s “Secret Speech” of February 1956, a closed-door denunciation of Stalin at the 20th Party Congress in Moscow. Chinese geneticists led by Tan Jiazhen, a Caltech-trained Drosophila researcher, used the Qingdao Genetics Symposium that same year to argue publicly for Mendelian inheritance--the first time anyone had done so since the founding of the People’s Republic. 

Mao’s 1958 “Eight-Point Charter of Agriculture,” directly influenced by Lysenko’s doctrine of close-planting and deep ploughing, contributed to the catastrophic crop failures of the Great Leap Forward, which is estimated to have caused 15-45 million deaths.[[cite:cas-history-soviet-model]][[cite:lysenkoism-wikipedia]][[cite:alphahistory-lysenko-china]]

Two projects emerged from this period nonetheless. The first was the total chemical synthesis of bovine insulin. Three years after Frederick Sanger had cracked the first full protein sequence (bovine insulin), total synthesis was the clear scientific frontier--and the perfect endeavor for demonstrating Communist China's scientific superiority. Proposed at a 1958 symposium under Wang Yinglai, the project divided more than two hundred synthetic steps among three institutions. 

On 17 September 1965, they observed crystallization of the first artificially synthesized, fully bioactive crystalline protein in human history. The accomplishment was Nobel-caliber, but the Communist government regarded the Nobel Prize as a symbol of bourgeois decadence. As was the mandate of the Cultural Revolution, Wang spent much of the next decade on house arrest within his own institute and was forced to attend endless sessions pontificating on Mao Zedong Thought.[[cite:bcas-insulin-2024]][[cite:synthetic-insulin-wikipedia]]

The second was artemisinin. On 23 May 1967, the People’s Liberation Army launched Project 523, a top-secret military effort to find a treatment for the chloroquine-resistant malaria killing Chinese and Vietnamese troops in the jungles of Southeast Asia. The military framing put the project under PLA protection, exempting it from the Cultural Revolution’s wider attack on academic science. 

Tu Youyou, a 38-year-old researcher at the [[entity:cacms|Academy of Traditional Chinese Medicine]] trained in both modern pharmacy and classical Chinese pharmacology, was appointed to lead the herbal-medicine team in January 1969. Her group screened candidates from classical Chinese texts and folk medicine; qinghao (青蒿, Artemisia annua, “sweet wormwood”), prescribed in Chinese medicine for intermittent fevers for some 1,500 years, showed real but inconsistent activity against malaria parasites. The unlock came from a 4th-century manuscript: Ge Hong’s Zhouhou Beijifang prescribed soaking qinghao in cold water rather than boiling it, which told Tu that the active ingredient was heat-labile and her standard ethanol-reflux extraction was destroying it. She switched to diethyl ether, which boils at 35°C, and the new extract produced complete inhibition in mouse and monkey malaria models. Because there were no functioning clinical-trial regulations, Tu and two colleagues dosed themselves as healthy volunteers to check for acute toxicity before exposing infected patients; the team then moved to Hainan Island, where qinghao extract cleared parasitemia in days. The active compound, qinghaosu (青蒿素), earned Tu the Lasker Award in 2011 and the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2015.[[cite:lasker-artemisinin-2011]][[cite:tu-youyou-wikipedia]]

Such successes were exceptions in a half-century of state-led attacks on Chinese science. The Cultural Revolution (1966–1976) closed universities to new undergraduates between 1966 and 1969 and to graduate students through 1977; the academy’s budget was slashed to less than one-sixth of its 1965 level by 1967; records document 229 scientists killed or driven to suicide.[[cite:cas-at-70-nature]][[cite:uchicago-cultural-revolution-college]]

title: Rebuilding Science After Mao
body:
In March 1978, with Deng Xiaoping consolidating power, the National Science Conference declared the rehabilitation of science and technology a strategic priority.[[cite:national-science-conference-1978]]

The National Natural Science Foundation was established in February 1986 as the financial backing for China’s peer review system for research. Weeks later, four senior physicists, alarmed by Reagan’s Strategic Defense Initiative announced two years earlier, wrote to Deng arguing that “high-tech development is a matter of international competition for national power, and China cannot stay aloof”; he approved their proposal within two days. The resulting [[reform:863-program|863 Program]] named biotechnology one of seven national priorities.[[cite:nsfc-wikipedia]][[cite:863-program-wikipedia]][[cite:chinadaily-863-program]] The Torch Program followed in 1988 to industrialize the output, and a strategic basic-research program was added in 1997.[[cite:torch-program-china]][[cite:973-program-wikipedia]]

From the early 1990s, with the Tiananmen-era brain drain still bleeding talent abroad, the state began offering returnees, also known as haigui (“sea turtles”), labs, budgets, and faculty positions to come home. 

Domestic firms began to emerge in parallel. Major General Lou Dan founded [[entity:3sbio|Shenyang Sunshine Pharmaceutical]] in 1993 to make recombinant biologics affordable, launching the rhEPO (recombinant human erythropoietin) product [[entity:3sbio|EPIAO]] in 1998 as China’s first marketed erythropoietin;[[cite:3sbio-ir-3sbio-corporate-governance]] in 1995, former chemistry teacher Zhong Huijuan started [[entity:hansoh|Hansoh Pharmaceutical]] in Lianyungang on a generics base, a firm later tied through marriage to [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui]]’s Sun Piaoyang.[[cite:scmp-zhong-huijuan-hansoh-ipo]][[cite:fiercepharma-hansoh-hengrui-family]] In 1999, the [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]] was founded to claim China’s 1% share of the Human Genome Project, the international consortium then sequencing the entire human genome for the first time.[[cite:innovation-china-haigui-1994]]

title: Human Genome Project, Chinese CROs, and Science Parks
body:
On 9 September 1999 at “nine seconds past the ninth minute of the ninth hour,” Yang Huanming, Wang Jian, Yu Jun, and Liu Siqi formally established the [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]] as a non-governmental institute under the [[entity:cas|Chinese Academy of Sciences]]. The country’s “1% Project” (sequencing roughly thirty megabases on the short arm of human chromosome 3) was modest in scope but gave China a seat at the international Human Genome Project consortium table.[[cite:bgi-history-genomics-cn]][[cite:chgc-hgp-china-protein-cell]]

Eighteen months earlier, a Chinese-born and Columbia-trained scientist Ge Li who'd been a founding scientist at a company called Pharmacopeia, a combinatorial chemistry company based in New Jersey, returned to China. He saw the potential of the tech, especially in combination with China's highly skilled and cheap scientist workforce, and set up [[entity:wuxi-apptec|WuXi PharmaTech]] in Shanghai’s Waigaoqiao Free Trade Zone. The original offering was simple combinatorial chemistry for Western pharma at a fraction of American labor costs--China's first domestic CRO. As Ge liked to put it, an entrepreneur could now begin a drug company “with a piece of paper, a pencil, and a credit card.”[[cite:ge-li-wikipedia]][[cite:cen-wuxi-li-words-2016]]

Shanghai’s Zhangjiang High-Tech Park, established as a national high-tech zone in 1992, was by the late 2000s emerging as China’s leading pharmaceutical R&D cluster, home to [[entity:roche|Roche]], [[entity:astrazeneca|AstraZeneca]], [[entity:novartis|Novartis]], and [[entity:gsk|GSK]] alongside a growing roster of domestic contract-research firms. State tax breaks pulled in the multinationals, and the scientists they trained then cycled out into local startups. Suzhou’s BioBAY opened in June 2007 as a startup-focused complement.[[cite:biobay-suzhou-baike]] The [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]] moved to Hangzhou in 2001 in exchange for municipal funding, then to Shenzhen in 2007,[[cite:ckgsb-bgi-gene-dreams]] where Yang Huanming was fond of remarking that “the mountains are high and the emperor is far away.” By 2010 BGI was the largest single producer of genomic data in the world.[[cite:bgi-shenzhen-nature-sequence-factory]]

title: BeiGene and the First Innovators
body:
By 2010, more than a hundred Illumina HiSeq sequencers, bought on a ten-billion-yuan China Development Bank loan, had made the [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]] the world’s largest single producer of genomic data, more than Harvard or the United States National Institutes of Health.[[cite:illumina-bgi-128-hiseq]] The companies that would carry Chinese drug innovation into the global pipeline took shape on the same upswing. 

[[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] was co-founded in Beijing in October 2010 by the American entrepreneur John Oyler and Xiaodong Wang, a Howard Hughes Medical Institute investigator at the University of Texas Southwestern and founding director of [[entity:nibs-beijing|NIBS Beijing]].[[cite:beigene-wikipedia]] [[entity:innovent|Innovent Biologics]] followed in Suzhou in 2011, deliberately licensing Adimab’s yeast-display platform (a leading Western fully-human antibody discovery technology) rather than building one in-house. [[entity:junshi|Junshi Biosciences]] (Shanghai, 2012) and [[entity:zailab|Zai Lab]] (Shanghai, 2014) emerged in the same wave.[[cite:innovent-biologics-wikipedia]][[cite:innovent-adimab-asianscientist]]

[[entity:hengrui|Hengrui Pharmaceuticals]], founded as a small generic-drug factory in 1970, had listed in Shanghai in 2000 and slowly transformed itself into one of the world’s most prolific clinical-trial sponsors.[[cite:hengrui-wikipedia]] The first generation of products from this cohort was largely “fast-follower” oncology, copying validated Western targets like [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s blockbuster Keytruda where the science was proven and the commercial upside enormous. The strategy minimized scientific risk but maximized crowding. At peak, nearly a hundred Chinese companies were racing in the same checkpoint-inhibitor space, and by 2020 China had become the world’s most prolific source of new combination trials targeting [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s Keytruda pathway.[[cite:globaldata-china-pd1-trials-2020]]

The 12th Five-Year Plan (2011–2015) elevated biotechnology to the status of a strategic emerging industry. In 2013, [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics]] vertically integrated by acquiring Mountain View–based Complete Genomics for $118 million, gaining its own sequencing-instrument platform that would later spin off as MGI.[[cite:bgi-complete-genomics-asianscientist]]

title: Regulatory Reform and International Markets
body:
Bi Jingquan’s Big Bang2015–2016

In January 2015, Bi Jingquan was appointed director of the [[entity:nmpa|China Food and Drug Administration]]. He was former vice-chair of the [[entity:ndrc|National Development and Reform Commission]], China’s top macroeconomic planning body, which gave him rare access to cross-agency leverage.[[cite:bi-jingquan-wikipedia]] Bi inherited an agency in crisis--roughly 22,000 drug applications were backlogged, and a CFDA self-audit that year had found that some 80 percent of submitted clinical-trial data was fraudulent or incomplete. In August, the State Council issued document No. 44, “Opinions on the Reform of Review and Approval Process for Drugs and Medical Devices.” [[reform:priority-review|Priority review]] followed in November; clinical-trial application timelines collapsed toward sixty working days; foreign clinical-trial data could be accepted without redundant local trials; and the agency was redirected explicitly at fraudulent data. Median new-drug-application review time fell from 200 working days to ~130.[[cite:ropesgray-china-state-council-44]][[cite:wang-2019-new-era-drug-innovation-china]]

ICH, 18A, and the first wins2017–2018

Before ICH membership, a Chinese clinical trial dossier wasn't directly usable by the FDA or EMA, and vice versa. Each agency demanded its own format, its own studies, its own definitions of safety and efficacy, meaning a drug developer aiming for global launch had to do most of the work twice. In June 2017, the [[reform:nmpa-rename|China Food and Drug Administration]] (renamed the [[entity:nmpa|National Medical Products Administration]] the following year in the government reorganization) was admitted as a regulatory member of the [[reform:ich-join|International Council for Harmonisation]], formally aligning Chinese drug regulation with American, European, and Japanese standards.[[cite:fda-cfda-ich-membership]][[cite:nmpa-2018-rename]] Such standardization laid the groundwork for the upcoming outlicensing boom.

On 30 April 2018, the [[entity:hkex|Hong Kong Stock Exchange]] introduced [[reform:hk-18a|Chapter 18A]] to its Main Board Listing Rules. Pre-revenue biotechs could now list provided they had at least one Core Product past the concept stage, an expected market capitalisation of one and a half billion Hong Kong dollars, twelve months of working capital, and meaningful pre-listing funding from a “Sophisticated Investor.”[[cite:charltons-chapter-18a]] The regime was explicitly designed to compete with the American Nasdaq for “China’s new generation of biotech companies”; annual listings under the rule would peak at twenty in 2021.[[cite:pharmaboardroom-hk-2021-record]] Combined with the [[reform:star-market|Shanghai sci-tech innovation board]] launched the following year, [[reform:hk-18a|Chapter 18A]] largely solved the valley-of-death financing problem (the gap between venture capital, which peaks around Phase I/II, and revenue, which arrives only after approval) that had plagued Chinese pre-revenue biotech.[[cite:xinhua-star-market-launch]]

ICH, 18A, and the first wins2017–2018

In December 2017, [[entity:legend|Legend Biotech]] (a GenScript spinout whose investigator-initiated LEGEND-2 trial in Xi’an had reported a 100% response rate in relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma at ASCO that year) signed an exclusive worldwide collaboration with [[entity:johnson-johnson|Janssen]] for its dual-VHH BCMA CAR-T cilta-cell. The upfront $350 million was, up until that point, the largest ever paid to a Chinese biotech for a single asset.[[cite:legend-janssen-collaboration-milestone]] 

Just weeks later, [[entity:junshi|Junshi’s]] [[entity:junshi|toripalimab]] became the first domestic checkpoint inhibitor approved in China;[[cite:frontiers-toripalimab-first-domestic-pd1]] [[entity:innovent|Innovent’s]] [[entity:innovent|sintilimab]], co-developed under a 2015 strategic alliance with [[entity:eli-lilly|Eli Lilly]] that gave Lilly ex-China rights.[[cite:innovent-lilly-tyvyt-press]]

title: Illegal CRISPR Babies
body:
The November 2018 announcement of the world’s first gene-edited human twins capped three years of Chinese research running ahead of international ethics consensus. In April 2015, Junjiu Huang at Sun Yat-sen University published the first report of CRISPR-mediated gene editing in human zygotes (non-viable tripronuclear embryos), an attempt to correct a β-globin mutation that causes thalassemia. The paper had been rejected by Nature and Science on ethical grounds before appearing in Protein & Cell,[[cite:liang-2015-crispr-tripronuclear]] and within weeks the International Summit on Human Gene Editing concluded that clinical use of germline editing would be “irresponsible.”[[cite:intl-summit-gene-editing-2015]] The following year, Chinese researchers conducted the first reported human trial using CRISPR-edited cells.[[cite:cyranoski-2016-first-crispr-human]]

On 25–26 November 2018, on the eve of the Second International Summit on Human Genome Editing in Hong Kong, the Shenzhen-based biophysicist [[reform:he-jiankui|He Jiankui]] announced the birth of twin girls whose CCR5 gene, the immune-cell receptor that HIV uses to enter T cells, had been edited at the single-cell stage to confer HIV resistance. Not only was the editing technically sloppy (the twins were genetic mosaics with off-target effects), the ethical-review documents were discovered to have been forged and the consent forms were lackluster.[[cite:wikipedia-he-jiankui-affair]]

The case closed in December 2019 with a three-year prison sentence for “illegal medical practice.”[[cite:statnews-he-jiankui-sentenced]] Beijing then pushed through a substantive legal overhaul: the [[reform:hgr-2019|2019 Regulations on Human Genetic Resources]], the 2020 Civil Code and [[reform:biosecurity-law|Biosafety Law]], and the 2020 eleventh amendment to the Criminal Law (which criminalized illegal human gene editing with up to seven years’ imprisonment) collectively reset bioethics governance.[[cite:covington-hgr-regulation]][[cite:china-org-civil-code-1009]][[cite:covington-biosecurity-law]][[cite:loc-criminal-law-amendment-11]]

title: Brukinsa, CoronaVac, and the mRNA Gap
body:
In February 2019, [[entity:henlius|Henlius’s]] [[entity:henlius|Hanlikang]] (a copy of Roche’s blockbuster anti-CD20 oncology biologic rituximab) became the first biosimilar approved in China.[[cite:henlius-hlx01-approval-press]] [[entity:beigene|BeiGene’s]] kinase inhibitor [[entity:beigene|zanubrutinib]], marketed as [[entity:beigene|Brukinsa]], won accelerated American approval in November, the first cancer drug developed in China to clear the United States [[entity:fda|Food and Drug Administration]].[[cite:fiercepharma-brukinsa-fda-approval]] [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui’s]] checkpoint inhibitor [[entity:hengrui|camrelizumab]] launched in May;[[cite:camrelizumab-first-approval-springer]] [[entity:beigene|BeiGene’s]] [[entity:beigene|tislelizumab]] won a Hodgkin-lymphoma indication in December.[[cite:beigene-tislelizumab-press-2019]] On 31 October 2019, [[entity:amgen|Amgen]] paid $2.7 billion for a 20.5% stake in [[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] plus China commercial rights to three of its marketed oncology drugs and a 20-asset pipeline co-development pact, marking the first time a top-ten multinational bought into a Chinese biotech as a strategic anchor rather than as a portfolio investor.[[cite:amgen-beigene-collab-2019]]

In 2018, [[reform:changsheng-scandal|Changchun Changsheng was fined roughly nine billion yuan for falsification in rabies-vaccine production]].[[cite:chinadaily-changsheng-fine]] In direct response, the [[reform:vaccine-admin-law|Vaccine Administration Law]] of 2019 imposed whole-process responsibility on producers across development, manufacture, distribution, and post-marketing.[[cite:nmpa-vaccine-administration-law]]

Then came COVID-19. By February 2020, parallel vaccine-development tracks ran across at least five technology platforms: inactivated, recombinant subunit, adenovirus-vectored, attenuated influenza-vectored, and messenger-RNA. [[entity:sinovac|Sinovac’s]] [[entity:sinovac|CoronaVac]] and [[entity:sinopharm|Sinopharm’s]] [[entity:sinopharm|BBIBP-CorV]] would receive [[entity:who|World Health Organization]] Emergency Use Listing in 2021 and ship into dozens of low- and middle-income countries.[[cite:who-sinovac-eul]][[cite:who-sinopharm-eul]] The messenger-RNA story, however, was a strategic embarrassment. The [[entity:walvax|Walvax]]-[[entity:abogen|Abogen]]-Academy of Military Medical Sciences candidate [[entity:walvax|ARCoV]] reached Phase III in Mexico and Indonesia by mid-2021 but never received broad authorization in time.[[cite:scmp-arcov-phase3-mexico-indonesia]] mRNA had been identified by Chinese planners as a strategic platform of the future, and failing to develop one ceded the most consequential biotechnology of the decade to American firms. The most consequential platform of the pandemic was developed and commercialized first by [[entity:pfizer|Pfizer]]-[[entity:biontech|BioNTech]] and [[entity:moderna|Moderna]].

Even so, 2020 became a record-breaking year for Chinese biotech financing.[[cite:nature-biotech-financing-2020]] Chinese healthcare startups raised roughly RMB 190 billion (~$28 billion), more than double the 2019 level. The combined market capitalisation of China-originated biotechs listed on STAR, HKEX, and Nasdaq rose from $3 billion in 2016 to $140 billion by year-end 2020, a 47-fold expansion in four years. Hong Kong alone hosted six of the year’s largest biotech IPOs, including [[entity:remegen|RemeGen]] ($515M), [[entity:simcere|Simcere]] ($461M), and [[entity:everest|Everest Medicines]] ($451M). Most tellingly, Chinese biotechs signed a record twelve major out-licensing deals with multinational pharmaceutical companies, carrying a median deal value above $900 million, clear validation that Chinese innovation was producing globally competitive therapies.[[cite:mckinsey-china-biopharma-dawn-2021]]

title: From Copycat to Global Supplier
body:
The value turn2021–2022

By 2021 the strategy that had built the innovator generation had become a trap. The [[entity:nhsa|National Healthcare Security Administration]], the cabinet-level agency the State Council carved off from three other ministries in March 2018 to consolidate insurance and drug pricing. [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui’s]] [[entity:hengrui|camrelizumab]] eventually entered the [[reform:nrdl-resume|National Reimbursement Drug List]] at deep discounts; the centralized [[reform:vbp|volume-based procurement]] mechanism (the state’s monopsony bulk-purchase scheme that demands deep discounts in exchange for reimbursement-list inclusion) turned would-be blockbuster checkpoint inhibitors into commodity goods almost on contact.[[cite:eversana-pd1-nrdl-2020]] In 2021, the regulator’s [[entity:cde|Center for Drug Evaluation]] issued formal [[reform:value-oriented-onc|Guidelines for Clinical Value-Oriented Oncology Drug Development]]: a quiet acknowledgement that fast-following [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s Keytruda was no longer a winning strategy.[[cite:pharmadj-cde-clinical-value-guidelines]]

Fosun Kite began local manufacture of axicabtagene ciloleucel (Yescarta, the Kite-Gilead CAR-T therapy for B-cell lymphomas) in China in June 2021;[[cite:kite-fosun-first-cart-china]] [[entity:jw-therapeutics|JW Therapeutics’]] [[entity:jw-therapeutics|relmacabtagene autoleucel]] followed in September as the first domestic engineered T-cell therapy approved.[[cite:jw-relma-cel-press]] On 28 February 2022 the [[entity:fda|FDA]] approved [[entity:legend|Legend Biotech]]’s [[entity:legend|Carvykti]] (ciltacabtagene autoleucel), partnered with [[entity:johnson-johnson|Janssen]]. Carvykti was the first Chinese-discovered cell therapy to clear the American market, and the first Chinese drug to compete on a novel construct (a dual-VHH BCMA design) rather than as a fast-follower; it validated the LEGEND-2 dataset that had triggered the $350M Janssen upfront five years earlier.[[cite:fda-carvykti-approval-2022]][[cite:fiercepharma-jj-legend-carvykti-fda-2022]] By 2022, Chinese companies were running 1.5 such programs for every one in American development. As recently as 2014, the United States had developed or co-developed more than half of all newly identified candidates in the class.[[cite:pharma-tech-cart-china-superpower]]

The out-licensing pivot2021–2024

The [[reform:fyp14-bioeconomy|14th Five-Year Bioeconomy Plan]], issued in 2022, broadened the national agenda from biopharma narrowly to medical health, bio-agriculture, bioenergy, bio-information, and biosecurity.[[cite:chinadaily-bioeconomy-plan]] China’s share of global biotech venture capital had climbed from 3.5% in 2010 to 18.9% by 2020[[cite:itif-china-biotech-2024]][[cite:nsf-skti-24-2022]]. The cycle then reversed sharply, driven by a global post-COVID healthcare valuation reset, US interest-rate hikes pulling capital back to American assets, and accelerating US-China geopolitical tensions that prompted foreign investors to withdraw.[[cite:stat-china-biotech-foreign-pullback-2024]] Chinese biopharma primary-market financing fell from a 2021 peak of roughly RMB 88 billion (about $13.6 billion) across 625 transactions to RMB 27.7 billion (about $3.8 billion) in 2024;[[cite:biopharmaapac-china-h1-2025]] Hong Kong biotech listings under [[reform:hk-18a|Chapter 18A]] dropped from twenty in 2021 to eight in 2022 to seven in 2023.[[cite:pharmaboardroom-hk-biotech-2022]] Companies that could no longer raise at home increasingly raised abroad through licensing. In August 2021, [[entity:remegen|RemeGen]] (a Yantai-based dual-platform biotech founded in 2008 by returnee scientist Jianmin Fang) granted [[entity:seagen|Seagen]] exclusive ex-China rights to disitamab vedotin, [[entity:nmpa|NMPA]]-approved as [[entity:remegen|Aidixi]] two months earlier as the first homegrown Chinese ADC, in a deal worth up to $2.6 billion (then the largest oncology out-license ever struck by a Chinese biotech).[[cite:businesswire-seagen-and-remegen-announce-exclusive-wo]][[cite:remegen-jianmin-fang]] [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s December 2022 expansion of its antibody-drug-conjugate pact with [[entity:kelun-bio|Kelun Biotech]], worth up to $9 billion,[[cite:merck-kelun-adc-deal-press]] was a second signal that out-licensing was becoming the new financing.

By 2024 China was no longer a downstream consumer of Western drug innovation but an upstream supplier of it. The total deal value of innovator-drug licensing agreements with Chinese biopharma licensors surged from $16.6 billion in 2023 to $41.5 billion in 2024, with Chinese assets accounting for roughly twenty-eight percent of all in-licensed innovator drugs at large pharma. Antibody-drug-conjugate licensing alone reached $10 billion.[[cite:globaldata-china-licensing-2024]] [[entity:hansoh|Hansoh Pharmaceutical]] alone signed two back-to-back [[entity:gsk|GSK]] ADC license-outs in late 2023 (HS-20089 (B7-H4) in October and HS-20093 (B7-H3) in December, each with headline value above $1.5 billion), then closed a [[entity:merck-co|Merck]] deal for the oral GLP-1 HS-10535 in December 2024 worth up to $2 billion.[[cite:gsk-hansoh-hs20089]][[cite:gsk-hansoh-hs20093]][[cite:biospace-merck-hansoh-2b-glp1]] China’s 2024 out-licensing transactions totalled ninety-four deals worth $51.9 billion. Upfront payments from those deals, around $3.1 billion in October alone, for the first time exceeded primary-market biotech listing proceeds in China.[[cite:biopharmaapac-china-h1-2025]][[cite:scmp-china-out-licensing-2025]] Chinese biotech’s share of global venture capital had collapsed from a 2021 peak to roughly 7% by 2024,[[cite:biocentury-europe-2024-vc]] making out-licensing not just a complement but the primary capital channel.[[cite:nature-china-licensing-2025]]

HARMONi-2 and the apex2024–2025

In September 2024, [[entity:akeso|Akeso’s]] Phase III HARMONi-2 trial of [[entity:akeso|ivonescimab]], a bispecific antibody targeting both an immune checkpoint and a tumour-vasculature growth factor, reported a forty-nine percent reduction in the risk of disease progression or death versus [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s Keytruda monotherapy.[[cite:akeso-harmoni2-wclc-2024]][[cite:iaslc-harmoni2-2024]] Summit Therapeutics had licensed the molecule in 2022 for $500 million upfront and up to $5 billion in milestones.[[cite:summit-akeso-2022]] It was the first time a Chinese-discovered molecule had decisively beaten [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s thirty-billion-dollar-a-year flagship in a head-to-head trial. Two months later, [[entity:merck-co|Merck]] hedged its position by signing a $3.3-billion deal for [[entity:lanova|LaNova’s]] LM-299, a same-class PD-1/VEGF bispecific in earlier development.[[cite:merck-lanova-2024]]

Two trajectories collided in 2025: AI-discovered drugs from Chinese labs went mainstream, while American policy moved decisively to wall the two biotech ecosystems off from each other. China’s outbound deals in 2025 reached a record one hundred and fifty-seven transactions worth roughly $135.7 billion (more than double 2024),[[cite:scmp-china-out-licensing-2025]] with American drugmakers signing fourteen China-asset deals worth $18.3 billion in the first half alone.[[cite:gabi-h1-2025-china-deals]] Roughly a third of all big-pharma licensing spending in 2025 sourced from China.[[cite:biopharmadive-china-licensing-tracker-2025]] The single largest single-asset transaction was [[entity:3sbio|3SBio]]’s May 2025 license-out of its in-house PD-1/VEGF bispecific SSGJ-707 to [[entity:pfizer|Pfizer]], worth $1.25 billion upfront and up to $6 billion all-in, the largest single-asset out-licensing by a Chinese biotech to that point.[[cite:fiercebiotech-pfizer-pays-3sbio-1-25b-for-pd-1xvegf-bi]][[cite:pfizer-pfizer-completes-licensing-agreement-wit]]

title: BIOSECURE and the BeOne Pivot
body:
House passageSeptember 2024

The [[reform:biosecure|BIOSECURE Act]] passed the [[entity:us-congress|United States House of Representatives]] 306 to 81 in September 2024 but failed to fold into the fiscal-year 2025 National Defense Authorization Act. The bill was driven by U.S. national-security concerns about Chinese biopharma access to American patient genetic data and the Beijing Genomics Institute’s documented links to the People’s Liberation Army.[[cite:ropesgray-biosecure-house-2024]][[cite:mwc-biosecure-house-2024]] The bill, which would restrict American executive-agency procurement and federal grants from “biotechnology companies of concern,” automatically captured the [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]] and three of its affiliates through the Pentagon’s so-called 1260H list of Chinese military companies (the Defense Department’s annual roster of entities tied to China’s military-civil fusion strategy, mandated by Section 1260H of the FY2021 National Defense Authorization Act).[[cite:foley-biosecure-2024]] [[entity:wuxi-apptec|WuXi]] divested its American medical-device testing operations and its Oxford Genetics and WuXi Advanced Therapies cell-and-gene-therapy units, citing direct client deterrence.[[cite:wuxi-namsa-2025]][[cite:wuxi-altaris-atu-2024]][[cite:yicai-wuxi-altaris-2024]] By the time the year closed, China was producing more innovative approvals (eighty-two since 2022, by the regulator’s count[[cite:nmpa-82-innovative-2024]]) and exporting more pipeline value than ever before.

Signed into law and the BeOne pivotDecember 2025

The [[reform:biosecure-enacted|BIOSECURE Act]] was signed into law on 17 December 2025 as Section 851 of the fiscal-year 2026 National Defense Authorization Act.[[cite:lw-biosecure-becomes-law-2025]][[cite:arnold-porter-biosecure-law-2025]] [[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] rebranded as [[entity:beigene|BeOne Medicines]] and redomiciled from the Cayman Islands to Basel: a Swiss-neutral home that signaled corporate independence from any single state without ceding to American jurisdiction either.[[cite:beone-launch-bw-2025]][[cite:beone-wikipedia]] The company that had been the canonical American-Chinese hybrid biotech was actively distancing itself from China as a corporate identity, even as it remained, by every operating metric, the same firm. American venture firms, which had backed many of the major Chinese biotech rounds at the 2021 peak, had largely withdrawn from China deals by 2025.[[cite:stat-china-biotech-foreign-pullback-2024]]

title: Rentosertib, DoveTree, and the AI Stack
body: The next frontier-validation signal came from outside Chinese pharma altogether. [[entity:insilico|Insilico Medicine]] published positive Phase II data for [[entity:insilico|rentosertib]], a TNIK-inhibitor for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis that the company describes as the first drug fully (target, molecule, and trial design) discovered and designed by artificial intelligence to clear that bar;[[cite:insilico-rentosertib-press-2025]][[cite:nature-medicine-tnik-rentosertib-2025]] [[entity:xtalpi|XtalPi]] listed under Hong Kong’s new Chapter 18C (a 2023 listing rule for pre-revenue specialist-technology companies including AI and robotics, distinct from the biotech-focused 18A track) and signed a deal reportedly worth as much as $10 billion with DoveTree, an AI-drug-discovery company founded by Harvard chemist and serial biotech entrepreneur Gregory Verdine.[[cite:xtalpi-18c-listing-2024]][[cite:xtalpi-dovetree-final-2025]][[cite:xtalpi-dovetree-loi-2025]] The frontier-language-model ecosystem opened up by DeepSeek, the Chinese AI lab whose late-2024 open-weights releases reached frontier capability at far lower training cost than US peers, began feeding domestic protein and genomic foundation models.[[cite:deepseek-wikipedia]]
